content.problemStatement:
Johnny has figured out that there are some numbers which have an interesting property: they are the same when read from left to right, and from right to left. For example, 5115 and 929 are such numbers, while 31 and 125 are not. Johnny calls such numbers palindromic numbers.

After a while, Johnny has realized that his definition of palindromic numbers is not really precise. Whether a number is palindromic or not depends on the base in which the number is written. For example, 21 is not palindromic in base 10 but it is palindromic in base 2 (because 21 = 101012).

Johnny finds it interesting that any number becomes palindromic when it is written in an appropriate base.

Given a number N, write a program to help Johnny compute the smallest base B such that N is palindromic when written in base B.
constraints: Base B must be an integer and B ≥ 2
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer t (1 ≤ t ≤ 1000), the number of test cases. Each of the next t lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^10).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with the smallest integer base B (B ≥ 2) such that the representation of N in base B is a palindrome.

sampleTestCases.explanation: N=1 → smallest base is 2 (single digit). N=4 → base 3 gives "11" which is palindrome, no smaller base works. N=21 → binary (base 2) representation is 10101, palindrome.
N=3 → base 2 representation "11" is palindrome, so answer 2. N=2 → no base ≤ sqrt(2) works, smallest base giving a single digit is 3.
N=6 → bases 2‑4 give non‑palindromes, base 5 yields "11". N=9 → binary "1001" palindrome, answer 2. N=15 → binary "1111" palindrome, answer 2. N=31 → binary "11111" palindrome, answer 2.

solution.approach:
For a given N we look for the smallest base B ≥ 2 that makes the representation of N a palindrome.
1. Single‑digit representations are palindromes. The smallest base that yields a single digit is B = N+1, but we are looking for the *smallest* base, so we first try smaller bases.
2. If the length of the representation is at least 3, then the base B must satisfy B ≤ √N (otherwise B³ > N). Therefore we can safely test every base B from 2 up to ⌊√N⌋ and directly check whether the digits of N in base B form a palindrome.
3. If no base in that range works, the representation must have length 2. A two‑digit palindrome has the form "aa", i.e. N = a·(B+1) with 1 ≤ a < B. The smallest such base is obtained with a = 1, giving B = N‑1, which is always ≥ 2 for N ≥ 3. (Special cases N = 1 and N = 2 are handled separately.)
The algorithm therefore:
- Handles N = 1 (answer 2) and N = 2 (answer 3) explicitly.
- Loops B = 2 … √N, checking palindrome by extracting digits.
- If none succeeds, returns N‑1.
Time complexity per test case: O(√N · log_B N) ≈ O(√N). With N ≤ 10^10 this is at most 10⁵ operations, easily fast for t ≤ 1000.
Space complexity: O(log_B N) for the temporary digit array, i.e. O(64) integers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(√N) per test case, overall O(t·√N_max) where √N_max ≤ 10⁵.
